next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 4

By the time Tovin drove them up from the gate and into her eel house, Lale's skirts were stiff at the hem with river mud and her hands smelled of iron, weed slime, and the old coins of the dead. She had meant to keep hold of the ledger the whole walk back. She had meant to get it dry, turn the next route notes into a list, and go to sleep with something orderly in her head for once. Instead Tovin snatched the bundle from under her arm, set it on the highest shelf by the stove, and pointed the spoon at all five of them like a dock hook.

"If anybody says cut, saint, corpse, shrine, gate, or fate before I put food in you, I'll knock teeth loose. Boots off."

The room answered before any of them did. Wet leather hit the door plank. A chair leg scraped. Rain ticked on the patched tin over the smoke vent. Tovin's house had never been pretty. It was a low hot box nailed over the eel races, with smoke-black rafters, hooks full of drying nets, and an old market awning cut up to make curtains that never quite met in the middle. The air carried eel fat, pepper, old ashes, and the sweet rot from the troughs below. It smelled like work that had kept winning by one day at a time.

Tovin moved inside it as if the walls had been built around the width of her shoulders. She had wound a strip of blue cloth around the spoon handle so her burned fingers could grip it without swearing at the heat every few breaths. The cloth had once been part of a good shirt, and she had patched it three times instead of throwing it away. That was the kind of piety she believed in.

open_hooks_to_carry: The siblings clear the first gate by hand under monsoon pressure.
Each child must name something Sereya took from them before the sluice will answer.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...hildren who wanted to die picturesque.

Children. Nami almost laughed.

Daro was watching her too closely. "What did it want?" he asked in a near whisper.

Nami dried her arm. "Maybe it wanted naming."

"From you?"

Lale looked up from the closed ledger. "Don't feed this."

Iven said from his blanket, "Too late. It's already eating."

Nami sat back down on her mat and wrapped the rag around her forearm though she was not cut. The seed pressed against the warm hollow above her hip, hidden in her waistband. Its hard shape made the whole side of her body feel privately altered.

Nobody slept after that. Dawn came slow and colorless through the shutters, turning each face into something used and stubborn. They packed in silence mostly. Riva did not mention the voice again, which meant she was carrying it like weight. Daro moved with a terrible care, as if preserving pitch. Lale kept the ledger close and would not let anyone else touch the binding. Iven joked twice and both jokes died where they landed.

No one asked Nami what she had heard on the landing. No one offered what the voice had become in their own heads. They did not need to. The room had changed in the night. Reed Gate was no longer only a job waiting inland with Tovin and her iron teeth. The route had found the soft places in all of them and put its fingers there.

When Riva opened the door to the morning flood, the house gave one last settling groan under their feet, wood adjusting around water, old vows, and the dead below. Nami pressed her palm to her skirt and felt the seed answer with its cold, patient weight.
state_deltas_required: Tovin's insistence that Reed Gate will not move without physical work and emotional truth.
Reed Gate opens and Riva emerges as the practical center of the mission, giving the family proof that restoration is possible while breeding resentment.
